title: Chapter 8 — Writing Claude Prompts That Are Specifications
CHAPTER 8 —
WRITING CLAUDE PROMPTS THAT ARE SPECIFICATIONS
segments.body_html:
The easy explanation is that the second prompt was "more detailed." That is true but it misses the structural point. The

Chapter 8 — Writing Claude Prompts That — There is a formal tradition behind this. IEEE 830-1998, the canonical reference for software requirements specifications

beats.text: The easy explanation is that the second prompt was "more detailed." That is true but it misses the structural point. The second prompt is not a more detailed request. It is a different kind of thing. A request names a topic and trusts Claude to fill in the rest. A specification names the artifact, the constraints, the context, the success criterion, and the scope boundary , and leaves Claude nothing to fill in that wasn't authorized. There is a formal tradition behind this.

segments.label: 01 / 12 · HOOK

title: THE FIVE
ELEMENTS.
segments.body_html: The specific task→The invariants→The context→The output format→language models work better when they can grade themselves with something other than themselves

beats.text: The specific task is the one thing you are asking Claude to produce. Not a topic. Not an area. The artifact: the function name, the file path, the return type. "Write parse_grades(path: str) -> list in parsers/grades.py." That is one sentence with a definite answer. Either Claude produces that artifact or it doesn't, and either outcome is detectable in one look.

segments.label: 02 / 12 · THE FIVE ELEMENTS

title: WHY MEMORY
DOESN'T EXIST.
segments.body_html:
The five-element format rests on one fact about Claude that, once understood, reorganizes everything else.

Why Memory Doesn't Exist — Claude has no memory between prompts. Every prompt is read fresh. The conversation scrolling up your screen is, mechanic

beats.text: The five-element format rests on one fact about Claude that, once understood, reorganizes everything else. Claude has no memory between prompts. Every prompt is read fresh. The conversation scrolling up your screen is, mechanically, the context window the model re-reads from scratch every time you press Enter. A fact that dropped out of the context window in a previous turn does not exist for the model in this turn.

segments.label: 03 / 12 · WHY MEMORY DOESN'T EXIST

title: CONVENTIONS
segments.body_html:
·Python 3.11+, standard library only unless a dependency is justified in the SDD.
·snake_case for functions and variables; dataclasses for record types.
·Dates are ISO-8601 strings; empty CSV cells parse to None, never "".

beats.text: Python 3.11+, standard library only unless a dependency is justified in the SDD. snake_case for functions and variables; dataclasses for record types. Dates are ISO-8601 strings; empty CSV cells parse to None, never "".

title: GUARDRAILS
segments.body_html: Never modify files in data/; the sample inputs are fixtures. Never add a dependency without updating SDD.md first.

beats.text: Never modify files in data/; the sample inputs are fixtures. Never add a dependency without updating SDD.md first.

Where things are — Notice that every line in that file is a decision that would otherwise have to ride along in each prompt. With it in pla

beats.text: Data model: models/grade.py - Parser: parsers/grades.py Spec: SDD.md (the file format lives in §1.4) Notice that every line in that file is a decision that would otherwise have to ride along in each prompt. With it in place, Prompt 2 from the parser sequence below no longer has to say "standard library only" or "empty cells become None" , those are already in context, true on every turn.

segments.label: 06 / 12 · WHERE THINGS ARE

title: THE DANGEROUS
MIDDLE.
segments.body_html:
The failure mode worth most of your attention is not the prompt with zero specification. A zero-specification prompt fai

The Dangerous Middle — Three of five is enough to produce output that looks right. The student commits, moves to the next step, and discovers t

beats.text: The failure mode worth most of your attention is not the prompt with zero specification. A zero-specification prompt fails loudly , Claude produces something obviously wrong, the student knows immediately to re-prompt, and the cost is ten minutes. The dangerous failure is the prompt with three or four of the five elements. It feels complete. It compiles. It runs. It does most of the task. The defect is one layer in. Three of five is enough to produce output that looks right.

segments.label: 07 / 12 · THE DANGEROUS MIDDLE

title: THE SAME FORMAT, FIVE
PROMPTS DEEP.
segments.body_html:
Here is what the five-element format looks like sustained across a complete build sequence. Seth is building the grade p

The Same Format, Five Prompts Deep — Prompt 1 — Define the data model.

beats.text: Here is what the five-element format looks like sustained across a complete build sequence. Seth is building the grade parser from scratch: data model, parser, tests, a bug fix, then documentation. Five prompts. Each one is a complete specification. Each one builds on the previous without assuming Claude remembers the previous , the relevant facts are re-supplied or pointed at in each turn. Write the Grade dataclass in models/grade.py. Fields: student_id: str, assignment: str, score: float, out_of: float, submitted_at: datetime | None.

title: 🕰️ AI
WAYBACK MACHINE.
segments.body_html:
Norbert Wiener (1815–1852) wrote what we would today recognize as the first computer program — and, more pointedly for t

🕰️ AI Wayback Machine — Norbert Wiener, 1940s. AI-generated portrait based on a public domain photograph (Wikimedia Commons).

beats.text: Norbert Wiener (1815, 1852) wrote what we would today recognize as the first computer program , and, more pointedly for this chapter, the first specification. Her Note G to Menabrea's Sketch of the Analytical Engine (Lovelace, 1843) contains a precise indexed table of operations for computing the Bernoulli numbers on a machine that did not yet exist. The table names the variables, the operations on them, the order of operations, the dependencies between steps, and the expected outputs.
